title: Linux & Terminal
content:
## ⌨️ Commanding Your Machine: The Linux Terminal

Full-stack engineers spend a significant portion of their day in the **terminal** (also called the command line, shell, or CLI). It is faster, more powerful, and more automatable than any graphical interface. Crucially, **all production servers run Linux** — so you must know the terminal to deploy and manage real applications.

---

## 1️⃣ Navigating the File System

| Command | Full Name | Purpose | Example |
|---------|-----------|---------|--------|
| `pwd` | Print Working Directory | Show current location | `pwd` → `/home/mwero/projects` |
| `ls` | List | Show files and folders | `ls -la` (all + details) |
| `cd` | Change Directory | Navigate to a folder | `cd projects/my-app` |
| `cd ..` | | Go UP one level to parent | |
| `cd ~` | | Go to home directory | The `~` is always your home |

**Hidden files** (starting with `.`) are not shown by default. Use `ls -a` to reveal them.

---

## 2️⃣ File & Directory Operations

| Command | Purpose | Example |
|---------|---------|--------|
| `mkdir` | Create a directory | `mkdir src` |
| `touch` | Create an empty file | `touch index.html` |
| `cp` | Copy a file | `cp app.js app-backup.js` |
| `mv` | Move OR rename a file | `mv old.js new.js` |
| `rm` | Remove a file | `rm old.js` |
| `rm -rf` | Remove folder recursively | `rm -rf node_modules` |
| `cat` | Print file contents | `cat .env` |

> ⚠️ **`rm` is permanent.** There is no Recycle Bin — files deleted with `rm` are gone forever.

---

## 3️⃣ File Permissions (`chmod`)

Linux has a permission system for every file: **Owner**, **Group**, and **Others**. Each can have **Read (r=4)**, **Write (w=2)**, and **Execute (x=1)** permissions.

```bash
ls -l
# -rwxr-xr-- 1 mwero staff 4096 Jan 1 script.sh

chmod 755 script.sh   # Owner: rwx (7), Group: r-x (5), Others: r-x (5)
chmod +x script.sh    # Add execute permission for everyone
```

`chmod` = **Ch**ange file **mod**e

---

## 4️⃣ Superuser: `sudo`

Some operations require **administrator (root) privileges**. `sudo` (SuperUser DO) temporarily elevates your permissions:

```bash
sudo apt install nginx       # Install a package with root access
sudo systemctl restart nginx # Restart a service
```

Use `sudo` carefully — with great power comes great responsibility.

---

## 5️⃣ Reading Files

| Command | Purpose |
|---------|--------|
| `cat file.txt` | Print entire file contents |
| `head file.txt` | Show first 10 lines |
| `tail file.txt` | Show last 10 lines |
| `tail -f log.txt` | Follow a file in real-time (for live logs) |

`tail -f` is extremely useful for watching server logs as requests come in.

---

## 6️⃣ `grep` — Search Inside Files

`grep` searches for patterns in files:

```bash
grep "password" .env        # Find lines containing 'password'
grep -r "TODO" src/         # Search recursively in a directory
grep -i "error" logs.txt    # Case-insensitive search
grep -n "useState" App.tsx  # Show line numbers
```

---

## 7️⃣ Package Managers

| OS | Package Manager | Command |
|----|----------------|--------|
| Ubuntu/Debian | `apt` | `sudo apt install git` |
| macOS | `brew` (Homebrew) | `brew install node` |

---

## 8️⃣ Manual Pages (`man`)

Every Linux command has built-in documentation:

```bash
man ls      # Read the manual for 'ls'
man chmod   # Read the manual for 'chmod'
```

Press `q` to quit the manual.

---

## 9️⃣ Essential Shortcuts

| Shortcut | Action |
|----------|-------|
| `Ctrl + C` | **Kill** the currently running process (SIGINT) |
| `Ctrl + Z` | **Pause/background** a process |
| `clear` | Clear the terminal screen |
| `Tab` | Autocomplete commands and filenames |

---

## 💡 Summary

| Concept | Command |
|---|---|
| Current location | `pwd` |
| List files (all) | `ls -la` |
| Navigate | `cd path`, `cd ..`, `cd ~` |
| Create dir/file | `mkdir`, `touch` |
| Delete | `rm` (file), `rm -rf` (dir) |
| Permissions | `chmod 755 file` |
| Admin access | `sudo command` |
| Search in files | `grep 'pattern' file` |
| Manual | `man command` |
| Kill process | `Ctrl + C` |
| Package manager | `apt` (Linux), `brew` (macOS) |

questions.question_text: Which command is used to see the path of the current directory you are in?
How do you create a new directory named 'projects'?
questions.explanation: mkdir is the standard command to create directories in Linux.

questions.question_text: What does the 'sudo' command allow you to do?
options: Execute a command with administrative (root) privileges

questions.question_text: Which flag is used with 'ls' to see hidden files (files starting with a dot)?
How do you delete a non-empty directory?
questions.explanation: '-r' is recursive and '-f' is force. Use with caution!

questions.question_text: Which shortcut stops a currently running process in the terminal?
questions.explanation: Ctrl + C sends a SIGINT signal to terminate the process.

questions.question_text: What command reveals the manual/documentation for a specific command?
How do you change file permissions in Linux?
questions.explanation: 'chmod' (Change Mode) is used to set read/write/execute permissions.

questions.question_text: Which character represents the 'Home' directory in a file path?
questions.explanation: The tilde (~) is a shortcut for /home/username.

questions.question_text: How do you search for a specific string within a file from the terminal?
questions.explanation: grep searches for patterns in text files.

options: Goes up one level to the parent directory
questions.explanation: '..' refers to the parent of the current directory.

questions.question_text: Which command is used to create an empty file?
questions.explanation: touch creates a file if it doesn't exist or updates its timestamp.

questions.question_text: How do you see the last 10 lines of a file?
questions.explanation: The 'tail' command shows the end of a file.

questions.question_text: What is the purpose of the 'apt' or 'brew' commands?
options: To manage and install software packages
questions.explanation: These are package managers for Linux and macOS.

questions.question_text: Which command clears the terminal screen scrollback?
questions.explanation: 'clear' refreshes the view by pushing old content out of view.
